title: Fortress North America: Mexico’s Role in Securing the Region’s Economic Future
gist: In this brief abstract (originally published by CNAS), Diego Marroquín Bitar frames the 2026 USMCA review as the first real test of turning economic security into a regional North American framework, arguing Mexico's heavy dependence on Chinese inputs and lack of investment-screening/export-control tools make it the pivotal gap to fix.

themes.theme: USMCA review as an economic-security test case

insights.claim: Economic security has become the organizing principle of global trade policy, making the 2026 review of the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) the first major opportunity to build that logic into a formal regional framework.
insights.speaker: Diego Marroquín Bitar
insights.so_what: Frames the 2026 USMCA review as the near-term policy event where economic-security doctrine either becomes concrete regional rules or stalls — a catalyst to watch this year.
insights.quote: Economic security has become the organizing principle of global trade policy

insights.claim: Mexico is the largest U.S. trading partner and a manufacturing hub for U.S. firms, yet it depends heavily on Chinese industrial inputs and lacks the investment-screening and export-control tools that its USMCA partners (the U.S. and Canada) already have in place.
insights.speaker: Diego Marroquín Bitar
insights.so_what: Mexico's missing screening/export-control infrastructure is the specific USMCA-review gap to watch — its resolution (or lack of it) will determine North American supply-chain de-risking credibility.
insights.locator: Mexico sits at the center of the challenge

insights.claim: The paper's central argument is that building credible North American institutions for investment screening, export controls, and supply-chain risk management will determine whether the region can be simultaneously open internally and selective/standards-based at its external perimeter.
insights.speaker: Diego Marroquín Bitar
insights.so_what: Sets the specific policy design test for the USMCA review — a 'two-tier' fortress model (open inside, selective at the border) rather than blanket protectionism or blanket openness.
insights.quote: open and dynamic inside the bloc and selective and standards-based at its perimeter
insights.locator: This paper argues that building credible institutions for screening
